retrieved_docs.title: Clark's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clark's rule is a known pediatric medication dosing rule described in the medical literature that utilizes the patient's weight to calculate medication dosage. Other equations that utilize pediatric weight to calculate medication dosing include Salisbury's rule, Penna's rule, and the Body Surface Area rule. Other methods besides Clark's rule that utilize pediatric age to calculate radiopharmaceutical medication dosages used in nuclear imaging include Young's rule, Webster's rule, and Fried's rule. [1] [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Clark's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Clark's rule is an equation used to calculate pediatric medication dosage based on the known weight of a patient and a known adult dose of medication to be used. Clark's rule equation is defined as the weight of the patient in pounds divided by the average standard weight of 150 pounds (68 kg) multiplied by the adult dose of a drug to obtain the pediatric medication dose, as is demonstrated below: (Weight* divided by 150 lbs.) x Adult Dose** = Pediatric Dosage (Weight*** divided by 68 kg) x Adult Dose** = Pediatric Dosage *Weight of pediatric patient in pounds (lbs.) **Adult dose is the recommended dosage for adult medication use ***Weight of pediatric patient in kilograms (kg)

retrieved_docs.title: Young's Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Young's Rule is an equation used to calculate pediatric medication dosage based on patient age and the known recommended adult dose. The definition of Young's Rule is the age of the patient divided by the age added to twelve, all multiplied by the recommended adult dose. This formula appears below:

retrieved_docs.title: HCQM Two-Midnight Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The level of care provided to patients is not a one-size-fits-all approach but one that varies with the clinical presentation and the clinician's professional judgment regarding the expected treatment course. It is also essential for healthcare staff to understand that the "two-midnight rule" primarily applies to Medicare beneficiaries; private payers have their own screening criteria to determine the appropriate level of care.

retrieved_docs.title: Stark Law -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The term "referral" means a request for a specific or service by a physician for Medicare Part B services and/or a care plan that includes designated health services. Also, the term "financial relationship" includes investment interest, ownership, and compensation arrangements.

retrieved_docs.title: HCQM Two-Midnight Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: When determining medical necessity, utilization management professionals carefully evaluate the severity of an illness or condition and analyze the intensity of the required medical services. This thorough assessment allows the healthcare team to make informed decisions regarding the level of care most suitable for the needs of the patient.

retrieved_docs.title: HCQM Two-Midnight Rule -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Utilization management and the Healthcare Quality and Management (HCQM) teams are essential components of the healthcare system, working diligently to ensure patients receive appropriate and medically necessary care. The primary focus of these teams is evaluating and optimizing the utilization of healthcare resources while maintaining high-quality standards.

retrieved_docs.title: McNemar And Mann-Whitney U Tests -- Definition/Introduction -- McNemar test
retrieved_docs.content: The McNemar test is a non-parametric test used to analyze paired nominal data. It is a test on a 2 x 2 contingency table and checks the marginal homogeneity of two dichotomous variables. The test requires one nominal variable with two categories (dichotomous) and one independent variable with two dependent groups. Also, the two groups in the dependent variable must be mutually exclusive, i.e., cannot be in more than one group. The minimal sample size required for the McNemar test is at least ten discordant pairs. The formula for calculating the Chi-squared value for McNemar test appears in Image 1, where b is the false positive count, and c is the false negative count.

retrieved_docs.title: Young's Rule -- Definition/Introduction -- [Age / (Age + 12)] x Recommended Adult Dose = Pediatric Dose
retrieved_docs.content: Young's Rule can be applied quickly in situations where the current weight of the patient is unknown. Other approaches to pediatric dosing using age include the Webster and Fried rules. [1] The Clark Rule or the Body Surface Area Rule can be implemented if the weight of the patient is known. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Gas Laws and Clinical Application -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In this article, the gas laws will first be described, then applied to clinical situations with worked examples to demonstrate the importance of appreciating how changing temperature, volume, or pressure can affect the body.

retrieved_docs.title: Six Sigma Method -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the define phase, problems and issues within the process are outlined from both the business and client perspectives. This may include launching a team, gathering information, planning the project, and setting goals. For example, a project may define a medical error the organization wants to address. During the measure phase, further narrowing of the project focus occurs, and baseline data are collected. In the analyze phase, data are examined to help identify the root cause of an issue and help remove inefficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: Patient Self-Determination Act -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: IN THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES April 3, 1990 Mr. LEVIN of Michigan (for himself, Mr. SWIFT, Mr. MOODY, Mr. MCDERMOTT, and Mr. FAUNTROY) introduced the following bill; which was referred jointly to the Committees on Ways and Means and Energy and Commerce A BILL To amend titles XVIII and XIX of the Social Security Act to require providers of services and health maintenance organizations under the Medicare and Medicaid programs to assure that individuals receiving services will be given an opportunity to participate in and direct health care decisions affecting themselves. Be it enacted by the Senate and House of Representatives of the United States of America in Congress assembled, SECTION 1. SHORT TITLE. This Act may be cited as the `Patient Self Determination Act of 1990'. SEC. 2. MEDICARE PROVIDER AGREEMENTS ASSURING THE IMPLEMENTATION OF A PATIENT'S RIGHT TO PARTICIPATE IN AND DIRECT HEALTH CARE DECISIONS AFFECTING THE PATIENT.

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Dispensing -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The CSA is responsible for categorizing all medications into schedules. This CSA also sets the requirements to prescribe, dispense, store, and dispose of medications. Furthermore, this Act was expanded with the Combat Methamphetamine Epidemic Act of 2005 (CMEA).

retrieved_docs.title: Evidence-Based Medicine -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Level IIB evidence is from at least one well-designed case-control or cohort study. A randomized controlled study cannot effectively or ethically study all clinical questions.

retrieved_docs.title: Federal Regulation of Medication Dispensing -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: These acts defined the two classes of federally regulated medications: OTC and prescription medications. Ultimately, these acts established the criteria for labeling a medication as a prescription drug, with the following requirements: The use of such a drug is safe only under the supervision of a licensed practitioner due to its toxicity or method of use. All drugs categorized as prescription medications are subject to the new drug application (NDA) approval process under the FDAMA.

retrieved_docs.title: Evidence-Based Medicine -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: All clinical studies or scientific evidence can be classified into one of the above categories. The clinician must then use their professional, clinical experience to extrapolate the scientific evidence as it applies to the specific patient. Most clinical studies have specific inclusion and exclusion criteria and the specific population studied. More often than not, the patient being treated by the clinician will have one or more substantial differences from the population in the study. The medical provider must then use their clinical judgment to determine how the variations between the patient and the study population are important or not and how they affect applying the study results to the specific patient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 4 -- Community 2681
retrieved_docs.content: The term “komplikace” (complication) is described as being able to be qualified by specific conditions, and the descriptors “Allowed qualifier” and “can be qualified by.” indicate that certain entities are permissible qualifiers for this category. In particular, the complication category can be qualified by diabetic kidney disease (اعتلال الكلية السكري), by other complications of pregnancy, and by death (التموت). This framing underscores that diabetic kidney disease, pregnancy‑related complications, and mortality are recognized as valid examples of medical complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 0 -- Community 8

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 640
retrieved_docs.content: Midodrin and makrolidy are both classified under the category “Medicinal product categorized by structure,” indicating that they are grouped according to their chemical or structural characteristics. Within this structural classification, makrolidy are linked to a contraindicated class, specifically methylergonovin, meaning that the use of makrolidy is contraindicated in patients who are receiving or require agents belonging to the methylergonovin class. Recognizing that both midodrin and makrolidy share the same structural category helps clinicians consider class‑related properties, while awareness of the contraindication between makrolidy and methylergonovin is essential for safe prescribing and avoiding adverse drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 3 -- Community 1787
retrieved_docs.content: The clinical entity c.bevalling; misvorming, bekken (benig) denotes a pelvic malformation that can cause a difficult labor, as illustrated by the phrase Moeilijke bevalling door bekkenmisvorming, which is mapped to this concept. This condition is identified as an indication for specialized care or intervention related to labor and delivery, underscoring the importance of targeted obstetric management when pelvic malformation is present.

retrieved_docs.content: Acute and unspecified renal failure is described as being related to and possibly synonymous with كلاء الكليون السفلي, indicating that the two terms may refer to the same clinical concept.

Together, these entities form an integrated network of procedures, classifications, clinical conditions, and contraindications that guide clinicians in selecting appropriate induction methods, managing uterine contractility disorders, addressing anatomical challenges, and optimizing maternal and fetal outcomes in obstetrics and perinatal medicine.

Overall, the integrated framework unifies diverse drug classes through a structural‑analogue taxonomy, connects them to a diagnostic radiopharmaceutical, and provides explicit hypersensitivity contraindications, thereby supporting consistent clinical documentation, safe prescribing and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2861
retrieved_docs.content: The Pediatric Adverse Events Terminology encompasses a range of specific adverse‑event concepts relevant to children, and it includes the concept تدلّي الحبل السري (umbilical cord prolapse). By incorporating this particular adverse event, the terminology provides clinicians and researchers with a standardized term for identifying, reporting, and managing umbilical cord prolapse in pediatric patients, thereby supporting consistent communication and improved patient safety.
